lines.text: "There is a song in the stone. And if you listen, you can hear the math starting to fail." That's how this book opens, and honestly? I had to put it down for a second.
Right? Like, what does that even mean — the math starting to fail? But somehow you know exactly what it means. You feel it before you understand it.

It's this narrator whom perhaps we are yet to meet, uploading their story to us directly, telling us the stone is waiting for us. That's such a strange framing device. We're not observers here. We're implicated.

And then the story begins in the Comms Bay on Mairee — the Fold has gone silent, Earth is cut off, their only way home. Their only supply line. And Commander Astrid Vilde is standing in a room full of people who've stopped breathing.

That moment killed me. "Breathe, dammit. We'll figure this out." And the bay remembered to breathe again. That's leadership under pressure, but it's also this beautiful metaphor for how much these people depend on her.
And then that eerie message from the Brisinger, the last ship that went into the Fold as it collapsed. I actually shuddered reading it.
Because we don't know what's happened to that ship. It's crew. We just know it's corrupted, it's wrong.
Oh, someone's definitely boarding it. That's a typical haunted spaceship trope. But something tells me this isn't going to be a cliché.
Nope. Can we talk about Ulre Corbin? Because I get weird vibes from that dude.
Yeah. The glove-pulling thing! Finger by finger.

He wants the Emergency Suppression Protocol activated immediately. And Astrid reads him perfectly — she says he's calculating, that the confrontation isn't over, that he's bypassing her entirely. She knows a coup is coming.
See, I read him slightly differently. I think he might genuinely believe he's right. Three thousand people, limited resources, every beat they wait costs something. He's not wrong about the math.

Oh, he's absolutely not wrong about the math. But Astrid says something crucial: "My judgment accounts for the trust among the settlers." She understands that survival isn't just resources. It's social cohesion. Panic kills faster than starvation.

And then the assembly happens, and she gives this speech. She spots this poor kid. Lost his father in transit. Something tells me were going to learn more about him. Then Astrid Vilde makes them chant "together." She's building a religion in real time.
Until the Guardians show up.
The lights go out, the Eyewyns drop, and there's this gravitic suppressor that makes the air thick. Heavy boots advancing in silent rhythm. Who sent them? Was it Ulre?
The timing is too perfect. Astrid literally just called the assembly. Corbin must have been one step ahead.

The boy, though. Astrid remembers promising him that no life given to the void would be forgotten. And when she makes her promise to the crowd — "the rest of the fleet will follow soon" — she knows it might be a lie.
She calls it necessary. That's a leader who's already compromising her integrity to hold things together. And that boy's icy stare? He knows. He sees through her.
He's going to be important. You don't linger on a character like that for nothing.

What strikes me too is the worldbuilding through time units. Pulses, beats, thuds, clicks — these people don't live by Earth time anymore. They live by the rhythm of machines. They've literally synchronized their bodies to ship gravity cycles. They feel time.
And it enables them to adapt to their new home. Mairee.
And their last home has stopped calling.
So here's my question going into the next chapter: Is the Fold closed because something broke it, or because something came through?
And what's up with the Brisinger?
Probably something that will keep me up at night.
Yuh. Sweet dreams.
